title: Array Aggregation: Sum, Average & Count
description: Learn the accumulator pattern to compute totals, averages, and conditional counts from an array.
congratsText: Great work! You now know how to aggregate array data using the accumulator pattern.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Traverse an array to compute a running sum
  • Compute an average from a sum and element count
  • Count elements that meet a condition
  • Apply the accumulator pattern to different aggregation tasks

sections.title: The Grocery Receipt Idea

quiz.question: What everyday process does summing an array most resemble?
options: A cashier scanning items one at a time and adding to a running total
A librarian alphabetizing books on a shelf
A teacher assigning random seats to students
A printer choosing which page to print first

sections.lesson:
The Grocery Receipt Idea
A cashier's total doesn't appear all at once — it grows one item at a time as each price is scanned and added. Summing an array works the same way: a running total that grows with each element visited.

sections.title: The Accumulator Pattern

quiz.question: Why must sum be set to 0 before the loop begins?
options: So the loop starts adding from a known, empty starting point
Because C requires every variable to start at exactly 0
To make the loop run exactly 5 times instead of more
It isn't required — sum works the same without it

sections.lesson:
The Accumulator Pattern
An accumulator is a variable initialized before a loop, then updated on every pass. It's the single most reusable pattern in array processing.
int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { sum += arr[i]; // same as: sum = sum + arr[i]; }
If sum started at some other value, or wasn't reset each run, the total would be wrong from the very first addition.

sections.title: Computing Sum

quiz.question: What is the value of sum after this loop runs on int arr[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40};?

sections.lesson:
Computing Sum
Summing is the accumulator pattern in its purest form: start at 0, add every element as the loop passes over it.
int arr[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40}; int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) { sum += arr[i]; } printf("Sum: %d", sum); // Sum: 100

sections.title: Computing Average

quiz.question: Given a completed sum and a known array size, how is the average computed?
options: Divide the sum by the number of elements
Divide the number of elements by the sum
Add the sum to the number of elements
Multiply the sum by the number of elements

sections.lesson:
Computing Average
Once the sum is known, the average is just one more step — no second loop needed.
float average = (float) sum / size; // casting to float avoids integer division truncating the result
Without the (float) cast, dividing two ints in C truncates any decimal portion, silently producing a wrong answer like 25 instead of 25.75.

sections.title: Counting with a Condition

quiz.question: What does this loop count?
options: How many elements in the array are greater than 50
The sum of all elements greater than 50
The index of the first element greater than 50
How many elements are equal to exactly 50

sections.lesson:
Counting with a Condition
The accumulator pattern isn't only for totals — it also counts. Instead of adding a value, add 1 whenever an element meets a condition.
int count = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { if (arr[i] > 50) { count++; } }
Swap the condition and this same shape counts anything: values above average, negative numbers, or matches to a target.

sections.title: Combining Sum, Average & Count in One Pass

quiz.question: Why can sum, count-above-threshold, and other aggregations often be computed in a single loop?
options: Because each element is already being visited once during traversal
Because C combines all loops into one automatically at compile time
Because arrays store pre-computed totals inside themselves
It isn't possible — each aggregation always needs its own loop

sections.lesson:
Combining Sum, Average & Count in One Pass
Since every element is already visited during traversal, multiple accumulators can update together in the same loop — no need to loop through the array twice.
int sum = 0, count = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { sum += arr[i]; if (arr[i] > 50) count++; }

sections.title: Complete Example: Sum, Average & Above-Average Count

quiz.question: In a program that needs the average BEFORE counting values above it, why might two separate loops be necessary?
options: The average isn't known until the sum loop finishes, so counting needs a second pass
C does not allow more than one loop in the same function
Average and count must always use different array variables
A single loop can never contain an if statement

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: Sum, Average & Above-Average Count
Some aggregations depend on a result that isn't ready until the first loop completes — like counting values above the average.
int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) sum += arr[i]; float avg = (float) sum / 5; int aboveCount = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { if (arr[i] > avg) aboveCount++; }
This is a normal, expected pattern — not every aggregation can be squeezed into a single pass.

sections.title: Best Practice: Testing Against Multiple Datasets

quiz.question: Why is it good practice to test an aggregation program against several different datasets, not just one?
options: Edge cases like all-equal values can reveal bugs a single test set hides
C requires at least three test runs before a program will compile
Running multiple datasets makes the program execute faster
It's only necessary if the array size is larger than 10

sections.lesson:
Best Practice: Testing Against Multiple Datasets
A program that works on one sample array can still hide bugs. Try datasets with all the same value, all zeros, or one very large outlier — these edge cases often expose accumulator mistakes that a 'normal-looking' dataset never triggers.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What is the core idea shared by sum, average, and conditional count?
options: All three reuse the same accumulate-while-traversing pattern
All three require the array to be sorted first
All three only work correctly on arrays of exactly 5 elements
All three need a separate function call for every element

sections.lesson:
Recap
Sum, average, and conditional count all grow from the same accumulator pattern: start with a known value before the loop, update it on every pass, and read the final result once traversal is done.
int sum = 0, count = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { sum += arr[i]; if (arr[i] > threshold) count++; } float avg = (float) sum / n;
